title: BPF integration into Linux memory management faces obstacles
summary: At the 2026 LSFMM+BPF summit, Roman Gushchin and Shakeel Butt led sessions identifying obstacles and requirements for using BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter) to control memory management heuristics, such as out-of-memory handling and NUMA balancing. If successful, BPF-based memory management could allow flexible, user-defined policies without kernel changes, potentially improving performance and adaptability for diverse workloads. Key obstacles include out-of-tree BPF programs, inability to attach struct ops programs to control groups, and safety/fallback concerns where a broken BPF program could crash the system.
context: The Linux Storage, Filesystem, Memory Management & BPF Summit (LSFMM+BPF) is an annual invitation-only event where kernel developers discuss improvements. BPF allows running sandboxed programs in the kernel, but its use in memory management is still experimental. Previous proposals for BPF-controlled memory heuristics have not been merged into the mainline kernel.

references.title: Linux Storage, Filesystem, MM & BPF Summit | LF Events
